mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the Seychelles on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa (officially), but the Seychelles is a small island nation 1,500 km east of mainland Africa, in the western Indian Ocean 🌊

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Seychelles?
mapQuestions.answer: Victoria, on the main island of Mahé. One of the smallest capital cities in the world (about 26,000 people), with a colourful clock tower (a small copy of London's) in the centre.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Seychelles speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Three official languages: Seychellois Creole (the main spoken language), English and French. Most Seychellois grow up speaking all three.

mapQuestions.question: How many islands make up Seychelles?
mapQuestions.answer: 115 islands! Most are tiny and uninhabited. Just three main islands (Mahé, Praslin, La Digue) have most of the population and most of the tourism.

mapQuestions.question: How big is the country?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 100,000 people live across all 115 islands, making Seychelles the smallest country in Africa by population. But the ocean territory (the "Exclusive Economic Zone") is 1.3 million square km, bigger than France.

foods.name: Grilled fish
foods.description: Whole snapper, kingfish or red emperor caught that morning, grilled over coconut-husk coals with lime, garlic and chilli. The Seychelles classic.

foods.name: Kari koko
foods.description: Seychellois coconut curry, made with fish, octopus or chicken, with onion, ginger, garlic and a Creole spice mix. Served over rice.

foods.name: Octopus salad
foods.description: Boiled octopus chopped finely with lime juice, onion, chilli, garlic and parsley. A signature Seychelles starter.

foods.name: Ladob
foods.description: Sweet plantain and sweet potato simmered in coconut milk with sugar and vanilla. Eaten as a dessert. Vanilla pods grow in Seychelles itself.

foods.name: Bonbon koko
foods.description: Sweet little coconut treats: rolled coconut sugar candy in pink, white and brown layers. Sold at small island markets.

foods.name: Coconut water
foods.description: Fresh young coconuts cracked open at the beach with a machete. Sweet, cold, the best thing on a hot Seychelles afternoon.

scavengerItems.label: A pink-granite boulder beach (Anse Source d'Argent on La Digue is the most famous)
A coco de mer palm (the world's biggest seed grows only in Seychelles)
A giant Aldabra tortoise (some are over 100 years old, common on Curieuse Island)
Bright reef fish on a Seychelles snorkel
Hermit crabs scuttling across a beach at dusk
A coconut vendor at a beach with a machete
The little Victoria clock tower (a tiny copy of London's "Little Ben")
A traditional Seychellois pirogue (a small wooden outrigger fishing boat)
The bright turquoise water of the Cousin or Cousine Islands marine reserve
A Seychelles black parrot (only found on Praslin)
A whale shark on a guided snorkel off Mahé (Aug–Nov)
A "moutya" drumming circle (an Afro-Creole rhythm tradition, UNESCO-protected)

geoMatches.description: The main island, with the small colourful capital Victoria, the international airport, and a spine of forested mountains down the middle. Most travellers start here.
The second island, an hour's boat from Mahé. Home to the Vallée de Mai UNESCO World Heritage forest, the only place coco de mer palms grow wild.
A tiny island where almost nobody has a car (everyone cycles). Home to Anse Source d'Argent, frequently voted one of the most beautiful beaches in the world.
A remote ring-shaped coral atoll, home to around 100,000 Aldabra giant tortoises, the largest population on Earth. Reachable only by expedition cruise. UNESCO World Heritage.
A small island just off Praslin, used as a tortoise sanctuary. Hundreds of giant tortoises roam free on the trails. Easy day trip with snorkelling.
A protected bird sanctuary, with thousands of seabirds (terns, noddies, frigatebirds) nesting on a single small island.

senses.smell: Coconut and vanilla on the sea breeze, salty Indian Ocean spray, smoky coconut-husk fires, fresh fish being grilled at a beach restaurant, frangipani flowers at sunset…
senses.hear: "Bonzour!" "Mersi!", Creole songs from radios, palm leaves rustling in trade winds, distant outrigger boat engines, the slap of waves on granite boulders, kingfishers calling…
senses.taste: Sweet coconut milk in fish curry, lime-sharp grilled snapper, fresh young coconut water, sweet pink-and-white bonbon koko candy, ripe mango cut on a beach…
senses.feel: Warm Indian Ocean water (around 28°C all year), powder-soft sand, the smooth bumps of pink granite boulders, the cool of a Vallée de Mai forest, the surprising weight of a coco de mer seed (up to 25 kg)…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is the coco de mer?
stories.body: The coco de mer is the largest seed in the entire plant kingdom: a double-lobed coconut weighing up to 25 kilograms, the size of a small child. It only grows on two Seychelles islands (Praslin and Curieuse). The tree takes 25 years to mature and the seeds take 6 to 7 years to ripen. Each tree is either male or female. For centuries, when these giant seeds washed up on Indian Ocean beaches after island storms, sailors thought they came from underwater forests in the deep ocean, and they were considered magical. The double-lobed shape was associated with paradise and fertility. Today the coco de mer is so protected that every seed has a unique serial number engraved on it before it can leave the country. The Vallée de Mai forest is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A seed bigger than a baby grows on only two islands on Earth. What other things have evolved to live in just one tiny corner of the world?

stories.question: How old are the Seychelles tortoises?
stories.body: Aldabra giant tortoises are some of the oldest animals on Earth. Many live 100 to 150 years, and some have been documented over 200. They are also enormous: adults weigh up to 250 kg (similar to a small fridge). There are around 100,000 of them living wild on Aldabra Atoll, one of the most remote spots in the world, plus protected populations on Curieuse and Cousin islands. The Seychelles tortoises survived the arrival of humans and sailing-ship hunters in the 1700s and 1800s because Aldabra was so far away. The famous Galápagos tortoises, by contrast, were nearly wiped out by sailors. On Curieuse, you can walk past tortoises lounging on the beach as you go to swim.

stories.question: How is Seychelles Creole made?
stories.body: Seychellois Creole ("Kreol Seselwa") is the everyday language of the islands. It mixes French (Seychelles was French from 1756 to 1814), English, and pieces of African, Malagasy and Tamil from the families who came to the islands as labourers and settlers. The grammar is much simpler than French, and the words look like phonetic spellings: "thank you" is "mersi" (from French "merci"), "good day" is "bonzour" (from "bonjour"). Children are taught in Creole, English and French in school. Creole was officially recognised in 1981 and is the language of Seychellois music, family conversation, jokes, and politics. About 90% of the population speaks it as a first language.

stories.question: Why are Seychelles beaches different from other tropical beaches?
stories.body: Most tropical beaches around the world are flat white sand with palm trees. Seychelles beaches have something extra: huge, ancient, pink-and-grey granite BOULDERS. Some are the size of small houses, weathered into smooth curves by 750 million years of wind and water. The Seychelles islands are the only mid-ocean granite islands on Earth (most other oceanic islands are volcanic, like Hawaii or Galapagos). The granite is a leftover piece of the ancient supercontinent Gondwana, which broke apart 200 million years ago. Anse Source d'Argent on La Digue, with its boulders, palms and pink-tinged sand, is regularly voted the most photographed beach in the world.

stories.question: What is "moutya"?
stories.body: Moutya is a Seychellois Creole musical tradition: drum-heavy, percussive, with deep call-and-response singing. It was created by enslaved Africans brought to the islands by French and English colonisers in the 1700s and 1800s, mixing African rhythms with the Creole language. Moutya was originally a secret evening gathering on the beach, played around bonfires, with the goatskin drum (also called "moutya") warmed over the fire before playing. Colonial authorities tried to ban it for a long time. After independence in 1976, Seychellois reclaimed moutya proudly. UNESCO put it on the "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2021. You can sometimes catch a moutya circle at a hotel or community event in the evenings.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Seychelles so far?
Beach, forest, snorkel, tortoise island, which has felt the most "Seychelles"?
Did you find a coco de mer seed (in a shop, since you cannot pick wild ones)?
How are the Seychelles beaches different from any other beaches you have been to?
Seychelles' Creole language was created by enslaved Africans mixing many traditions. How does language carry history?

wordSearch.hint: Seychelles foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Aldabra giant tortoise
animals.description: The world's biggest tortoise population lives on Aldabra Atoll (100,000+). Protected populations on Curieuse, Cousin and around Praslin let you walk among them.

animals.name: Seychelles black parrot
animals.description: A dark coffee-coloured parrot found ONLY on Praslin island. The national bird, only about 500 left.

animals.name: Tropical butterflies
animals.description: Dozens of species across Praslin's Vallée de Mai forest.

animals.name: Reef fish
animals.description: Snorkel anywhere off Mahé, Praslin or La Digue and you will see clownfish, parrotfish, surgeonfish, butterflyfish in their hundreds.

animals.name: Hawksbill sea turtle
animals.description: Nests on Seychelles beaches between October and February. Cousin Island has one of the densest hawksbill populations on Earth.

animals.name: Whale shark
animals.description: The biggest fish on Earth visits Seychelles waters every August to November. Guided snorkel encounters are possible off Mahé.

animals.name: Seychelles flying fox (fruit bat)
animals.description: Big fruit bats with 1 m wingspans glide between palm trees at dusk. The locals call them "Sousouri".
